summary: Patrick McKenzie demystifies tax filing for micro-ISV (uISV) owners, arguing that the process is far simpler than most aspiring entrepreneurs fear. He walks through his actual 2006 tax forms for Bingo Card Creator, showing that only four forms were needed and the entire process took about three hours. His Schedule C-EZ shows $2,480 in gross receipts, $1,225 in expenses, and $1,255 in net profit, with self-employment tax of $177. He acknowledges that complex situations like AMT or home offices would warrant hiring an accountant, but for most small software businesses, taxes are straightforward.
key_points: Filing taxes for a small software business requires only a few basic forms (Schedule C-EZ, Schedule SE, 1040) and simple arithmetic
The IRS is actually 'decently taxpayer-focused' and forms are designed for a sixth-grade reading level, contrary to popular fear
Patrick's entire 2006 tax preparation for Bingo Card Creator took only three hours including research and data collection
Fear of tax complexity is an unnecessary barrier that stops people from starting small software businesses
Profitable uISVs should expect to pay estimated taxes in their second year of business
money_quotes: Many folks think you have to be superman to navigate the IRS' maze, when they are actually decently taxpayer-focused and charged by regulations to make things easy for someone with a six grade reading level.
Reports of the impossibility of taxes have been greatly exaggerated.
When I do, I'll pay an accountant to have the headache on my behalf.
Its more aimed at allaying the fears of folks who are thinking of starting a uISV, but worried that the IRS will steal their lunch money after making them fill out 10,000 pages of forms.

On the outside, counting the two blog posts, data collection, downloading forms, and reading up on the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion that I have to do every year, my 2006 taxes took me three hours to prepare.
key_insight: Tax filing for a small software business is far simpler than most people fear, requiring only basic arithmetic and a few forms, and should not be a barrier to starting a business.
